one_line_takeaway: VulSifter uses a 0-4 LLM score plus a test-code heuristic to strip noise from GitHub vulnerability-fixing commits, producing CleanVul (8,198 pairs, 90.6% label correctness) without NVD linkage; the dataset-quality gains are large, but downstream detector accuracy stays only marginally above chance.
key_finding: VulSifter (GPT-4 backend) reaches F1 0.82 at separating genuine vulnerability fixes from other VFC changes, the best of the five LLMs tested. Filtering lifts dataset Correctness from 28.7% (uncleaned) to 90.6% at threshold 3 (+215.6%) and 97.3% at threshold 4 (+239.0%) on the test sample, matching cited high-quality sets SVEN (94.0%) and PrimeVul (86.0%) at far larger scale than SVEN. Fine-tuning transfers: a CodeBERT model trained on CleanVul and tested on PrimeVul scores 58.09% accuracy, edging out PrimeVul's own intra-dataset 56.61% (CodeBERT) / 57.19% (best, GraphCodeBERT); CleanVul-trained models hit 64.87% on SVEN vs 55.75% for PrimeVul-trained. The empirical study finds ~80% of non-vulnerability VFC changes are test-related (41.2%) or bug fixes (38.2%).
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is VulSifter, described as the first automated approach to identify function-level vulnerability-fixing changes within vulnerability-fixing commits (VFCs) WITHOUT requiring NVD linkage, and CleanVul, the dataset it produces. VulSifter combines an LLM (prompted to emit a 0-4 confidence score that a diff is a genuine vulnerability fix) with a lightweight heuristic that pre-filters test-related changes by file/function naming conventions. Applied to a fresh crawl of GitHub (127,063 repos, 5,352,105 commits, 43,029 candidate VFC function changes), it yields CleanVul: 8,198 vulnerable/benign function pairs at score threshold 3 (90.6% label Correctness on a test sample) or 6,368 pairs at threshold 4 (97.3%). The stated motivation is that mainstream vulnerability datasets label every change in a VFC as vulnerability-related, giving 40-75% noise; VulSifter separates genuine fixes from co-committed test/bugfix/refactor changes. Note: prior high-quality datasets SVEN and PrimeVul are CITED baselines, not this paper's work; PrimeVul is the NVD-name-matching approach VulSifter positions itself as complementing.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model. This is a defensive data-quality paper. The problem addressed is label noise in security ML datasets: automatic keyword/NVD-based mining tags all diffs in a tangled VFC (e.g., the ThingsBoard XSS commit that also touches imports, license, and tests) as vulnerability fixes, poisoning the training signal for downstream vulnerability detectors. There is no modeled adversary, attack surface, or exploit; the 'harm' is degraded detector accuracy from mislabeled data. No threat model was invented for this review.
limitations: 1) Absolute detection accuracy is modest and the flagship 'generalization win' is razor-thin: 58.09% vs 57.19% is under a 1-point margin, and most cross-dataset accuracies sit at 50-58%, only slightly above the 50% random baseline the authors themselves cite. 2) The task is pre-fix vs post-fix (revised code is the 'benign' label), a known weak proxy for real vulnerability detection rather than detecting vulnerabilities in the wild. 3) Correctness figures (esp. 97.3%) are measured on a 487-item test sample, not verified over the full 8,198/6,368-function release. 4) Ground truth rests on manual labeling with Kappa 0.681 (substantial, not near-perfect), so both training labels and the correctness yardstick carry human noise. 5) The heuristic only removes test-related changes and keys on the English token 'test' / framework conventions, which is language- and convention-specific. 6) Despite six-language claims, the corpus is heavily Java (26,423 of 43,029 changes), and per-language balance is uneven (125 C++, 312 C#). 7) Correctness quantifies label cleanliness of the dataset, not whether trained models genuinely detect vulnerabilities.

datasets_used: CleanVul (this paper's dataset): 8,198 vulnerable/benign function pairs at threshold 3, or 6,368 at threshold 4; six languages (Java, Python, C, JavaScript, C#, C++)
GitHub crawl corpus (this paper): 127,063 repositories, 5,352,105 commits, 43,029 candidate VFC function changes (26,423 Java, 6,591 Python, 5,578 C, 4,000 JavaScript, 312 C#, 125 C++)
Manual correctness test sample: 487 function changes (95% confidence, +/-4.4%)
Manual LLM-evaluation set: 775 function changes across five labeling rounds, Cohen's Kappa 0.681
Uncleaned VFC dataset (baseline for RQ3): 28.7% correctness
quantitative_results: VulSifter (GPT-4) F1 for identifying vulnerability-fixing changes: 82.24% (best; GPT-3.5 75.85%, GPT-4o 74.73%, Gemini 1.5 Pro 71.77%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 70.91%)
Dataset Correctness after filtering: 28.7% -> 90.6% at threshold 3 (+215.6%) and 97.3% at threshold 4 (+239.0%), with heuristic
Threshold 1: 43.1% correctness with heuristic (+50.1%) vs 37.5% without (+30.6%); threshold 2: 49.4% with heuristic vs 57.7% without
Ablation F1 vs full 82.24%: w/o context 77.64% (-4.6%), w/o commit message 81.17% (-1.07%), w/o both 76.00% (-6.24%), w/o 0-4 output (binary) 74.66% (-7.58%), w/o heuristics 78.97% (-3.27%)
Cross-dataset (all languages): CleanVul-trained CodeBERT tested on PrimeVul 58.09% acc > PrimeVul intra 56.61% (CodeBERT) / 57.19% (best); tested on SVEN 64.87% > PrimeVul-trained 55.75%
Intra-dataset accuracy (all languages, GraphCodeBERT best): CleanVul 68.96%, PrimeVul 57.19%, SVEN 81.86%
Java-only intra-dataset: GraphCodeBERT on CleanVul 74.78%, CodeGPT 73.36%, CodeLlama-7B 70.21%
RQ3 clean vs uncleaned (GraphCodeBERT): intra-dataset 68.96% acc / 71.57% F1 (clean) vs 55.23% / 46.32% (uncleaned); on PrimeVul 54.65%/59.59% vs 52.42%/40.50%; on SVEN 62.07%/69.88% vs 52.19%/54.33%
Non-vulnerability change taxonomy (n=136): test-related 41.2% (56), bug fixes 38.2% (52), support changes 14.7% (20), refactoring 5.1% (7), documentation 0.7% (1)
baselines_compared: Dataset-correctness baselines: SVEN, PrimeVul, DiverseVul, CVEFixes, CrossVul, VulnPatchPairs, BigVul, CodeXGLUE, and the uncleaned VFC dataset
LLM vulnerability-fix detectors: GPT-3.5 (chatgpt-35-0301), GPT-4 (gpt-4-0314), GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06), Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro
Fine-tuned code models: RoBERTa-125M, CodeBERT-125M, GraphCodeBERT-125M, GPT-2-124M, CodeGPT-124M, CodeLlama-7B
Training-source comparison: models fine-tuned on CleanVul vs PrimeVul vs SVEN vs uncleaned data, evaluated cross-dataset
